mnemonics.title: FRAME ALLOCATION STRATEGIES
mnemonics.content: FIVE PIGS EAT GRASS: Fixed, Variable (Proportional), Priority, Global, Equal, Allocation

acronyms.expansion: Page Fault Frequency - measures how often a process experiences page faults
acronyms.usage: Used in PFF control algorithm to dynamically adjust frame allocation based on observed fault rates

acronyms.expansion: First In First Out - page replacement algorithm (not allocation, but related)
acronyms.usage: When a page must be evicted, FIFO removes the oldest page in memory

acronyms.expansion: Least Recently Used - page replacement algorithm
acronyms.usage: Evicts the page that has not been used for the longest time

acronyms.expansion: The set of pages currently active/being used by a process
acronyms.usage: OS tries to allocate enough frames to hold each process's working set to minimize thrashing

visualTips.concept: Relationship between frames and page faults
visualTips.visualization: Draw a curve showing page fault rate on Y-axis and number of allocated frames on X-axis. The curve drops sharply then flattens - this is the characteristic LAZY TAIL shape. Mark the THRASHING ZONE on the left where too few frames cause excessive faults.
Imagine a pizza shared at a table. GLOBAL = everyone shares from one large pizza and can take slices from others. LOCAL = each person gets their own pre-allocated plate they cannot share.
Use a simple ratio: (Process Size / Total Size) × Total Frames. Think of it like splitting a bill proportionally - everyone pays their fair share based on what they ordered.

Draw a process spending most time on the page swap path (disk to memory and back) with only tiny bits of CPU execution time. Show it like a person running back and forth to a library rather than reading.

analogies.analogy: Think of frames as parking spaces in a mall parking lot. Fixed allocation gives each car (process) a predetermined spot regardless of car size. Proportional gives more spots to larger vehicles. Priority gives better spots (closer to entrance) to VIP cars.

Your desk at work represents your working set - the books and papers you need RIGHT NOW. The library represents physical memory. Going to the library (page fault) happens when what you need isn't on your desk.

Imagine studying for an exam with your notes scattered across 10 different books. Every time you need to answer a question, you have to fetch a different book from different shelves - you spend more time fetching than studying.

GLOBAL = a shared fridge where anyone can take any food. Risk: one hungry person might take everything. LOCAL = each person has their own labeled shelf. Fair but may waste space if someone doesn't use their shelf.
